title: MRL Increment 7: Navigation & Discoverability
body:
# MRL Increment 7: Navigation & Discoverability

## Purpose
Make the Master Requirements List (MRL) a first-class, discoverable module in IMQ so that users and buyers can easily find it, understand its role, and see how it fits into the broader IMQ workflow.

This increment is intentionally focused on **UI navigation and user comprehension**, not data, governance, or backend behavior.

---

## Background

MRL Increments 1–6 established a technically complete, read-only Master Requirements List:

- Core domain model
- In-memory service
- Read-only API exposure
- UI rendering and client-side filtering

However, user feedback and demo usage revealed a key gap:

> Functional features without clear navigation and explanatory context are effectively invisible.

The MRL page currently exists but is only accessible via direct URL, limiting its demo value and making its purpose unclear to first-time users.

Increment 7 addresses this by improving **discoverability, navigation placement, and mental-model clarity**.

---

## In Scope

### 1. Navigation Exposure

Add visible navigation access to the Master Requirements List in the IMQ Web application. One of the following approaches should be implemented:

- Add **“Master Requirements”** as a top-level item in the left-hand navigation, **or**
- Introduce a **“Configuration”** (or similar) parent section with **“Master Requirements”** as a child item

The exact option can be chosen based on existing navigation structure, but the result must be:

- Easily discoverable without knowing the URL
- Consistent with how other first-class modules are exposed

---

### 2. Page-Level Explanatory Context

Add short, buyer-oriented explanatory copy to the MRL page that clearly communicates:

- What Master Requirements are
- Why they exist
- How they are used across IMQ

Example intent (not prescriptive wording):

> “Master Requirements define the approved, audit-ready expectations used across Jobs, Qualifications, and Assessments. They prevent free-text inconsistencies and provide a single source of truth for role requirements.”

This copy should:

- Be concise and readable
- Sit near the top of the page
- Improve understanding without introducing workflow or instruction complexity

---

### 3. Light Contextual Linking (Optional, Informational Only)

Where appropriate, add **non-interactive or informational references** from related areas (e.g., Jobs or Qualifications views) indicating that:

- Requirements are sourced from the Master Requirements List

Examples:

- Static helper text
- Tooltip copy
- Informational banners or labels

This is **not** a navigation rewrite or cross-module integration—only light context-setting.

---

## Explicit Non-Goals

To keep scope tight and avoid rework, the following are **out of scope** for this increment:

- Creating or editing requirements
- Governance workflows (approval, review, retirement)
- Bulk or realistic data seeding
- Backend or API changes
- Authorization or role-based access control

---

## Implementation Constraints

- Changes are limited to **IMQ.Web**
- No changes to Core, API, or services
- MRL remains strictly read-only
- No new routes or endpoints beyond existing UI routing

---

## Acceptance Criteria

This increment is complete when:

- A user can access the Master Requirements List via visible application navigation
- The navigation placement is intuitive and consistent with other modules
- The MRL page includes clear explanatory copy describing its purpose and role in IMQ
- No backend, API, or data behavior has changed
- The feature can be demonstrated without explaining URLs or internal architecture

---

## Outcome

At completion, Master Requirements should feel like a **core, intentional part of IMQ**, not a hidden or experimental feature—improving demo clarity, buyer understanding, and confidence without expanding functional scope.
